A broad mix of industries was on the list: Retail and media, property management and packaging, an investment firm and a couple of industrial conglomerates. Their fortunes almost added up to $100bn – equivalent to about 0.4% of the US’s GDP that year. The oligarchy has changed drastically since then. Bernard Arnault, of French luxury group LVMH, Amancio Ortega, the Spanish clothing mogul, and Warren Buffett, the US investor, were the only old-school billionaires among the top 10 in 2025. The rest largely made their money from high-tech: Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Ellison, Steve Ballmer and Google’s Sergey Brin and Larry Page. source_signals.title: Woman, 19, killed in Essex dog attack named as Jamie-Lea Biscoe
source_signals.summary: Police arrest man, 37, on suspicion of being in charge of dog dangerously out of control and causing injury resulting in death A 19-year-old woman who died after a dog attack in Essex has been named by police as Jamie-Lea Biscoe. Police said the victim was found with serious injuries after emergency services were called to a property in Leaden Roding at 10.45pm on Friday. Biscoe was pronounced dead at the scene. A 37-year-old man from Dunmow, who was arrested on suspicion of being in charge of a dog dangerously out of control and causing injury resulting in death, has been bailed until July while inquiries continue, Essex police said on Sunday. The canine, which was a family pet and believed to be a lurcher crossbreed, was seized and tests are under way to formally establish the dog’s breed, the force added. Assistant chief constable Stuart Hooper said: “Our thoughts remain with all those who knew and loved Jamie-Lea. Her young life has been so tragically cut short. “Our detectives are continuing to work around the clock to establish exactly what happened and specialist officers are continuing to support Jamie-Lea’s family. “This is unimaginable for her loved ones and friends and, as such, I would ask people to respect their grief and privacy at this extremely difficult time. “Our officers remain at the scene and anyone with concerns or information can speak with them there or contact us in the usual way.” A post-mortem examination is due to take place on Sunday, police said. Anyone with information that could assist the investigation has been asked to contact Essex police through their website or anonymously through Crimestoppers. On Thursday, a three-month-old baby died in a suspected dog attack at a property in Redcar, North Yorkshire. The baby girl is believed to have died as a result of a dog bite in the Dormanstown area and a woman, aged 31, was treated in hospital for an injury to her arm from a bite, police said. Armed officers destroyed one dog that had gone on to the street and a second recovered by police has since been destroyed. They say you have exceeded your storage plan, so documents are no longer being backed up, and photos you take aren’t being uploaded. You have been resisting Apple’s efforts to get you to pay a minimum of 99p a month for more storage. source_signals.title: ‘Irresponsible failure’: Google, Meta, Snap and Microsoft slam EU over child sexual abuse law lapse
source_signals.summary: Experts warn lapse could sharply reduce reports of abuse, echoing a 58% drop during a similar legal gap in 2021 Sign up for the Breaking News US email to get newsletter alerts in your inbox The European parliament has blocked the extension of a law that permits big tech firms to scan for child sexual exploitation on their platforms, creating a legal gap that child safety experts say will lead to crimes going undetected. The law, which was a carve-out of the European Union’s ePrivacy Directive, was put in place in 2021 as a temporary measure allowing companies to use automated detection technologies to scan messages for harms, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM), grooming and sextortion . However, it expired on 3 April, and the EU parliament decided not to vote to extend it, amid privacy concerns from some lawmakers. This article was amended on 10 April 2026 to correct the name of the EU’s ePrivacy Directive. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Super Mario what?! The seven best obscure Mario games
source_signals.summary: As The Super Mario Galaxy Movie storms the box office, we look back at the best forgotten games inspired by Tetris, Lemmings and … vitamins? It should be no surprise that the latest Super Mario movie is smashing box office records – despite the, let’s say mixed, reviews . Nintendo’s iconic plumber has been a pop culture staple for 45 years, starring in some of the bestselling video games ever made, from the original Donkey Kong through to the joyous Super Mario Bros Wonder and the chaotic Mario Kart World. But as with any storied showbiz career, there have been some lesser works. Who can forget – or actually remember – Hotel Mario, a door-shutting puzzle game for the doomed Philips CD-i console? Or what about Mario Teaches Typing, a 1992 educational game for the PC in which players navigate the Mushroom Kingdom by … correctly inputting words. Yet there have also been genuine treasures lost along the way. Here, then, are seven of our favourite much-overlooked Mario odysseys. Continue reading...

Especially as the 27-year-old from Crawley, the reigning Spanish Open champion, took a triple-bogey eight at the 2nd on Thursday. Not the most auspicious start to his Augusta National career, but he limited the first-round damage to 76, then shot 69 and 71. Sadly his final round isn’t going so well, and he’s just dumped two balls in the water at the iconic par-three 12th, the first spinning back off the bank, the second from the dropzone not even getting over to dry land before dunking into the drink. A quadruple-bogey seven. He isn’t the first, he won’t be the last, and things could have gotten a whole lot worse, just ask the Towering Inferno … Bogey at the last for Jon Rahm. lead_story.title: Trump's blockade threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
lead_story.summary: Donald Trump’s threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz raises the risk of escalation without resolving the underlying confrontation with Iran. The key question now is whether Tehran can absorb further pressure, and how long Washington is prepared to bear the economic and geopolitical costs of disruption to a vital global shipping route.
lead_story.source_signal_title: Trump's blockade threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
body: Donald Trump’s threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz has raised the stakes in the stand-off with Iran, but it does not solve the underlying problem.

The strait remains a vital route for oil and gas shipments, so any disruption would quickly rattle energy markets and shipping. But Iran can probably endure pressure for longer than Washington can tolerate the economic and strategic consequences of a prolonged shutdown.

That leaves the US facing an old dilemma: how to show strength without triggering a wider conflict. Tehran, meanwhile, can use the threat itself as leverage, even if it cannot safely close the waterway for long.
So the threat may add danger, but it does not change the basic balance.

stories.title: Djibouti's president wins unprecedented sixth term with 97.8% of vote
stories.summary: Ismail Omar Guelleh has extended his long rule after an election boycotted by the main opposition candidates. The result underlines the entrenchment of power in a small but strategically located state on the Horn of Africa.
stories.source_signal_title: Djibouti's president wins unprecedented sixth term with 97.8% of vote
body: Djibouti’s President Ismail Omar Guelleh has secured an unprecedented sixth term after winning 97.8% of the vote in an election boycotted by the main opposition candidates. The result extends his 27-year rule in a small state whose location at the mouth of the Red Sea gives it outsized strategic importance.

Mr Guelleh, 78, has long presided over a political system in which opposition activity has been tightly constrained and little real electoral competition has been available. The near-total victory, while not surprising, underlines how firmly power remains concentrated around the presidency.

Djibouti hosts foreign military bases and sits on a vital shipping route linking Europe and Asia to the Indian Ocean, making stability there a matter of wider concern. But the absence of meaningful contest also raises familiar questions about succession, political openness and how long the current governing arrangement can endure.

stories.title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
stories.summary: The failure of talks has left markets braced for a longer period of elevated oil and gas prices, with tankers still stranded in the Gulf. Inflation, transport costs and borrowing expectations are all vulnerable if the Strait of Hormuz remains constrained.
stories.source_signal_title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
body: The collapse of US-Iran talks has left energy markets braced for another period of instability, with tanker traffic in the Gulf still constrained and traders unsure how quickly conditions will normalise.

The Strait of Hormuz is central to the global oil trade, and even limited disruption can push up crude and gas prices. That filters quickly into transport, food and industrial costs, which is why the impact is being felt far beyond the region itself.

The bigger worry for markets is that a ceasefire or diplomatic pause would not immediately undo the damage already done. Businesses are still dealing with higher freight costs, tighter margins and the risk that borrowing costs stay elevated if energy prices remain volatile.

For governments and central banks, the problem is timing as much as scale. Even if the fighting eases, the economic shock can linger in supply chains and inflation data for weeks or months. That makes the next move in Washington and Tehran important not just for geopolitics, but for the wider outlook for growth and prices.

stories.title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
stories.summary: Donald Trump’s blockade threat raises immediate practical questions about enforcement, legality and the risks of confrontation at one of the world’s most important chokepoints. The answer matters for oil markets, shipping insurers and governments far beyond the Gulf.
stories.source_signal_title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
body: A naval blockade is an attempt to prevent ships entering or leaving a defined area by using military force or the threat of it. In the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most important oil chokepoints, that would mean intercepting or deterring commercial vessels moving between the Gulf and the open ocean.

In practice, enforcing a blockade would be highly risky. Warships, aircraft, mines and coastal weapons could all be involved, but so could boarding operations, convoy protection and warnings to shipping. The strait is narrow, heavily trafficked and close to Iranian territory, so any move to close it would create an immediate danger of escalation and a serious legal dispute over whether the action was lawful.

The stakes are large because the strait carries a substantial share of seaborne oil and fuel trade, and even the threat of disruption can move markets, insurers and freight rates. The wider uncertainty is whether any blockade threat is meant as pressure, as a negotiating tool or as a prelude to force; the practical and economic consequences would differ, but all would be felt far beyond the Gulf.

stories.source_signal_title: Tankers urged not to pay toll to Iran for use of strait
body: Tanker firms are being advised not to pay Iran any levies in return for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, as commercial shipping is drawn further into the confrontation around the waterway. The advice reflects concern that any payment scheme could be read as accepting Iranian authority over transit through a route that is crucial to global energy supplies.

The warning comes amid heightened tensions after Donald Trump said the US would begin blockading the strait, raising fresh alarm across the shipping industry. Even the threat of interference has already led some operators to slow sailings, review routes or hold back vessels until the security picture is clearer.

If ships stop transiting freely, the consequences would extend well beyond the Gulf. Higher freight costs, more expensive insurance and delays to oil and fuel deliveries would quickly feed into markets in Europe and Asia, while governments would face pressure to protect shipping without triggering a broader military clash.

stories.source_signal_title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
body: British Steel is once again at the centre of an argument about whether the state should step in to protect a strategic industry.

Labour MP Nic Dakin has said nationalisation may be the best outcome if ownership talks do not deliver a viable future for the company. His intervention reflects a broader concern about jobs, industrial capability and the long-term security of UK steelmaking.

The government has been open to intervening in difficult cases when there is a case for protecting critical capacity, but full state ownership would be a significant step. It would raise questions about cost, management and what kind of industrial strategy ministers are prepared to pursue.

For the workforce and the surrounding supply chain, the immediate issue is uncertainty. For ministers, the issue is whether British Steel is treated as a commercial problem or a national strategic asset.

The row matters because it involves a senior figure in a party that has built much of its appeal on attacking the conduct of established politicians and the wider political class. There is still a difference between a disputed filing problem and deliberate evasion, and the available reporting does not by itself establish criminal wrongdoing. But the size of the sum, and the prominence of the person involved, mean the episode is likely to remain politically awkward unless the party can provide a fuller explanation.

stories.source_signal_title: Starmer urges US and Iran to 'find way through' after peace talks falter
body: Sir Keir Starmer has urged the United States and Iran to find a way through their deepening dispute after negotiations faltered and tensions rose further.

The prime minister is trying to strike a careful line: supporting restraint while avoiding the impression that Britain is being drawn into the dispute on either side. That matters because the UK has long ties to Washington, but also interests in keeping Gulf shipping open and limiting the wider economic impact.

Britain’s leverage is limited, though not irrelevant. It can press for de-escalation through diplomatic channels and use its position with allies to encourage caution, but it cannot dictate the course of events if the confrontation hardens.
For Starmer, the test is whether Britain can remain a useful interlocutor without becoming entangled in a conflict it did not choose and cannot control.

stories.title: Deep-fried food banned in new plans for school dinners

body: Ministers are proposing tighter standards for school meals, including restrictions on deep-fried foods and sugary puddings. The plans are part of a broader push to improve nutrition in England’s schools and to reduce children’s exposure to food that is high in fat, salt and sugar.

The argument for the changes is straightforward: better school food can help children eat more healthily at a time when concerns over childhood obesity remain high. Supporters will say schools should be offering meals that set a benchmark for balanced diets, not simply reproducing cheaper convenience food.

But the practical questions are just as important. Schools will need to absorb any extra costs, adapt menus and ensure pupils will actually eat what is served. Parents and caterers are likely to ask whether the new rules are realistic in under-pressure school kitchens, and whether the policy will be backed by enough funding to make it work.

stories.title: Britain could adopt single market rules without MPs’ vote as part of UK-EU reset
stories.summary: Ministers are preparing legislation that could allow closer alignment with EU single market rules without a full parliamentary vote. The proposal is likely to sharpen political tensions over sovereignty, trade and the government’s post-Brexit strategy.
stories.source_signal_title: Britain could adopt single market rules without MPs’ vote as part of UK-EU reset
body: Ministers are preparing legislation that could allow Britain to align with EU single market rules without requiring a full parliamentary vote. The proposal would deepen the government’s planned reset with Brussels, but it also risks reopening one of the most sensitive arguments left by Brexit: how far the UK should accept rules shaped in Europe without direct domestic consent.

Supporters of closer alignment argue that the UK has already paid a high economic price for friction in trade, particularly for smaller exporters that face extra paperwork and regulatory divergence. They see the move as a practical way to reduce barriers and stabilise relations with the EU, rather than as a reversal of Brexit itself.

Critics will see something else: a transfer of influence over standards, made easier by executive action and harder for MPs to scrutinise. That tension between economic pragmatism and constitutional control is likely to define the political row if the legislation progresses, especially in a Parliament where post-Brexit sovereignty remains an emotive issue.

stories.title: Meta pulls Facebook ads recruiting for social media addiction lawsuits
stories.summary: Meta has removed ads that appeared to recruit users for lawsuits over social media addiction. The move comes against the backdrop of wider legal and public pressure on large platforms over alleged harms.
stories.source_signal_title: Meta pulls Facebook ads recruiting for social media addiction lawsuits
body: Meta has removed Facebook advertisements that appeared to recruit users for lawsuits over alleged social media addiction. The company’s intervention comes as large platforms face growing scrutiny over the effects of their products on children and teenagers, and over how legal claims are being organised around those concerns.

The ads sit at the intersection of public health concerns, litigation and platform moderation. Meta is already under pressure after losing a landmark social media addiction case in California, and any suggestion that its own services are being used to solicit claimants gives the company another reputational problem to manage.

What happens next will depend on whether the material breaches Meta’s advertising rules and whether similar campaigns are being run elsewhere online. The wider issue, however, is unlikely to fade: regulators, parents and lawyers are all pressing for clearer limits on how social media platforms design, promote and police engagement.

stories.title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
stories.summary: Major AI firms are trying to shape the public and political debate as criticism of the industry grows. The question is whether policy papers and thinktank partnerships can shift opinion, or simply underline how contested the sector has become.
stories.source_signal_title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
body: Major AI companies are spending heavily on policy work as public unease about the technology grows.

The push is not just about one report or one thinktank. It is about trying to shape the debate around jobs, safety, competition and regulation at a moment when AI firms are becoming more powerful and more visible in politics.

That in itself is a sign of the problem. Companies that once marketed themselves as neutral builders of useful tools now face suspicion over whether they will automate work too quickly, concentrate too much power or move faster than safeguards can keep up.

Funding policy papers may help these firms sound engaged and responsible, but reputational repair is harder than messaging. The deeper question is whether they can persuade governments and the public that their economic ambitions are compatible with tighter oversight and slower, safer deployment.

stories.title: Greece to ban social media for under-15s from next year
stories.summary: Greece is moving to restrict social media use for children under 15, following similar steps elsewhere in Europe. The policy raises familiar questions about enforcement, child protection and the role of platforms in young people’s lives.
stories.source_signal_title: Greece to ban social media for under-15s from next year
body: Greece is set to restrict social media use for children under 15 from next year, joining a growing European debate over how far governments should go in protecting young people online. The move follows similar efforts in countries including France and Spain, reflecting mounting concern about screen time, exposure to harmful content and the effect of platforms on mental health.

In principle, the aim is to make it harder for children to access services that are not designed for them. In practice, enforcement is the central challenge. Age checks can be bypassed, accounts can be shared and platforms are often better at collecting data than verifying who is using a device at any given moment.

The policy also raises broader questions about responsibility. Governments want action from tech firms, but parents, schools and app stores would all have roles in making such a ban workable. If Greece does proceed, it will become another test case for whether digital-age restrictions can be enforced without pushing children into less visible and potentially less safe corners of the internet.

stories.title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals

stories.source_signal_title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals
body: Manchester City’s latest win has tightened the Premier League title race and shifted the mood around the run-in.

Arsenal’s wobble has made the lead look less secure, while City have found form at the right time and appear to be building momentum again. In a title race this close, that combination can matter as much as the points themselves.

The key period now is the direct stretch between the front-runners and their closest challengers. If Arsenal drop more points, City have the experience and squad depth to make the pressure tell quickly.

For Arsenal, the concern is not just City’s quality but the psychology of a chase. They have to hold their nerve while rivals sense vulnerability, and that can become decisive long before the final whistle of the season.

stories.title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
stories.summary: Wout van Aert has won Paris-Roubaix after edging Tadej Pogacar in a dramatic sprint finish. It is one of the season’s biggest one-day racing results and a major marker for the cobbled classics.
stories.source_signal_title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
body: Wout van Aert has won Paris-Roubaix after edging Tadej Pogacar in a dramatic sprint finish, sealing one of the standout one-day results of the cycling season. The victory adds another major cobbled classic to Van Aert’s record and denies Pogacar a famous win in only his first appearance at the race.

Paris-Roubaix remains one of cycling’s most punishing tests, with rough pavé sectors often deciding the outcome long before the finish. In a race where bike handling and resilience matter as much as raw climbing talent, Van Aert’s ability to hold his nerve against one of the sport’s biggest names underlines why the event is so prized.

For Pogacar, the result will still be read as evidence that he can contend on terrain far beyond his usual strengths, even if the final sprint went against him. For the wider classics picture, it confirms that the spring remains open and unpredictable, with the biggest riders now converging on the hardest races.

stories.title: Sunderland sink Spurs in De Zerbi's opening game in charge

stories.source_signal_title: Sunderland sink Spurs in De Zerbi's opening game in charge
body: Roberto De Zerbi’s first match as Tottenham manager ended in a 1-0 defeat at Sunderland, leaving Spurs in the relegation zone. Nordi Mukiele scored the only goal in a result that will intensify pressure on a club already under severe strain.

The immediate significance is obvious: this was meant to be a fresh start, and instead it became another setback. De Zerbi will be judged not on long-term ideas but on whether he can quickly stabilise a side short on confidence and points, with every remaining fixture carrying relegation implications.

There is still time to recover, but little margin for error. Tottenham’s problems are no longer hypothetical; they are structural and immediate, with the club now forced to respond not just to a bad result, but to the prospect of a damaging slide that could define the season.

stories.title: Newcastle want Man Utd's Ugarte - Monday's gossip

body: Newcastle’s reported interest in Manchester United midfielder Manuel Ugarte leads Monday’s transfer gossip. For now, this should be treated as interest rather than a completed deal, with no confirmation that a move is close.

Ugarte would fit Newcastle’s need for extra control and energy in midfield, particularly if they are planning for another season that demands depth across domestic and European competitions. Manchester United, meanwhile, would have to decide whether they regard him as part of their long-term core or as a player they could cash in on.

As ever with early transfer talk, the key distinction is between a club being linked with a player and anything actually happening. The market is still fluid, and until bids, negotiations or more authoritative reporting emerge, this remains one to watch rather than one to trust as settled.
